R Notebook

@inproceedings{johnson-etal-2021-classical, title = “The {C}lassical {L}anguage {T}oolkit: {A}n {NLP} Framework for Pre-Modern Languages”, author = “Johnson, Kyle P. and Burns, Patrick J. and Stewart, John and Cook, Todd and Besnier, Cl{’e}ment and Mattingly, William J. B.”, booktitle = “Proceedings of the 59th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics and the 11th International Joint Conference on Natural Language Processing: System Demonstrations”, month = aug, year = “2021”, address = “Online”, publisher = “Association for Computational Linguistics”, url = “https://aclanthology.org/2021.acl-demo.3”, doi = “10.18653/v1/2021.acl-demo.3”, pages = “20–29”, abstract = “This paper announces version 1.0 of the Classical Language Toolkit (CLTK), an NLP framework for pre-modern languages. The vast majority of NLP, its algorithms and software, is created with assumptions particular to living languages, thus neglecting certain important characteristics of largely non-spoken historical languages. Further, scholars of pre-modern languages often have different goals than those of living-language researchers. To fill this void, the CLTK adapts ideas from several leading NLP frameworks to create a novel software architecture that satisfies the unique needs of pre-modern languages and their researchers. Its centerpiece is a modular processing pipeline that balances the competing demands of algorithmic diversity with pre-configured defaults. The CLTK currently provides pipelines, including models, for almost 20 languages.”, }

This is an R Markdown Notebook. When you execute code within the notebook, the results appear beneath the code.

Try executing this chunk by clicking the Run button within the chunk or by placing your cursor inside it and pressing Ctrl+Shift+Enter.

plot(cars)

Add a new chunk by clicking the Insert Chunk* button on the toolbar or by pressing Ctrl+Alt+I.

When you save the notebook, an HTML file containing the code and output will be saved alongside it (click the Preview button or press Ctrl+Shift+K to preview the HTML file).*

The preview shows you a rendered HTML copy of the contents of the editor. Consequently, unlike Knit, Preview* does not run any R code chunks. Instead, the output of the chunk when it was last run in the editor is displayed.*

Data Preparation


# install.packages("factoextra")
# install.packages("dplyr")
# install.packages("ggrepel")
# install.packages("tidyverse")

library(factoextra)
library(dplyr)
library(ggrepel)
library(tidyverse)
library(tidytable)
library(cluster)
library(combinat)

(For the above ggrepel library, see https://github.com/slowkow/ggrepel

setwd("~/GitHub/cltk-2025-atticus")

dims <- c("title", "author", "path", "form", "lemma", "tag", "Aspect", "Mood", "Number",
                        "Person",
                        "Tense",
                        "VerbForm",
                        "Voice",
                        "Case",
                        "PronType",
                        "Gender",
                        "Polarity",
                        "Degree",
                        "NumType")

data <- read.csv("atticus-study-results.csv", na.strings="", col.names = dims, stringsAsFactors = TRUE)

summary(data)

 (Other)                :3331083   NA's                  : 111453

  Tense         VerbForm        Voice              Case            PronType        Gender        Polarity      
 Abl :      1   Fem :      2   Act : 704181   Nom    : 965300   Prs    : 227646   Fem : 842793   Neg :  85918  
 Fut :  32881   Fin : 744897   Masc:      1   Acc    : 785675   Rel    : 222104   Masc:1190519   NA's:5191032

 Pqp :  15041   Masc:     17   NA's:4204460   Gen    : 308399   Ind    :  81172   NA's:2565680                 
 Pres: 469403   Neut:      2                  Dat    : 107181   Tot    :  35487                                
 NA's:4533646   Part: 323726                  (Other):   2136   (Other):  56141                                
                NA's:4038528                  NA's   :2462976   NA's   :4498938                                
  Degree        NumType       
 Abs :  31266   Card:  49595  
 Cmp :  52130   Dist:    888  
 NA's:5193554   Ord :  14756  
                NA's:5211711

One thing I want to note is that not every value is relevant. I’ll do this further below and not here, but I want to go one at a time and see whether Degree [c(“Abs”, “Cmp”)], Gender [c(“Fem”, “Masc”, “Neut”)], or Case (c(“Nom”, “Acc”, “Abl”, “Gen”, “Dat”, “Loc”, “Voc”, “Abs”)) make any difference. Some potential issues are also that there are redundancies in the variables. If we want to do significance testing, we want to know each variable is independent. So, if we get rid of POS tags, we should still keep SCONJ, ADP, CCONJ,

#Lets get rid of the non-Cicero works now, but save the Cicero works for comparison
data.with.cicero <- data
data <- data[rowSums(data == 'M. Tullius Cicero', na.rm = TRUE) == 0 | data$title == "Letters to Atticus",]

titles <- unique(data$title)
num_words <- table(data$title)
final.frame <- data.frame(title=titles)

Now that we have the data, what we’re really interested in is how many of each tag appears in each work (how many of each POS, how many perfect verbs, whatever). The point is, we’re not interested in every individual word.

This block of code will loop through and create a new data frame called final.frame (which is probably poorly named, but I’ll fix it later or I won’t). This data frame has a variable for the count of each tag, and every row is a unique work.


for (i in 1:length(titles)) {
  frame <- data[data$title == titles[i],]
  frame <- subset(frame, select= c(-form, -path, -NumType, -author, -lemma))
  for (n in 2:length(frame)) {
    t <- table(frame[,n])
    #t <- prop.table(t)
    names <- names(t)
    
    for (name_index in seq(1, length(names))) {
      final.frame[i, names[name_index]] <- unname(t[names[name_index]])
    }
  }
}

#Normalize by number of words in each work
for (i in 2:length(final.frame[1,])) {
  for (n in 1:length(final.frame[,1])) {
    final.frame[n,i] <- final.frame[n,i] * (10000 / num_words[[final.frame[n,]$title]])
  }
}

A few things will make our life easier. We don’t want any NAs in there, because that’ll gum up the clustering. We’ll replace any missing values with 0, because 0 occurrences of a variable is the only reason there would be NA values at this stage.

I have a hopefully temporary measure where I replace row 46’s title with Apocolocynthosis. The title is given as “Petronius. Seneca Apocolocynthosis” because it’s based off a Loeb edition of that name. However, when I abbreviate the names at the end of this block, this makes it ambiguous with the other Perseus text which contains just Petronius, and rownames() doesn’t allow duplicates.

I also remove Cicero’s works other than the Letters to Atticus. The reason is that the clustering algorithm greatly prefers to put works by the same author together. This is great, but we’re not interested in the authorship of the Letters. We’re interested in how similar it is to other texts of the same genre. So, Cicero has to go.

The numbers aren’t normalized according to the size of their respective works yet, so I’ve divided each number by the total number of words in the given work in the next chunk. I multiplied each by 100,000 too, just to get numbers that look a little nicer and aren’t in scientific notation.

Finally, we do what I said above, turning the rownames into the title. I shorten them to 12 characters max.


#Removed punctuation because PCA showed that it was an "important" variable
final.frame <- subset(final.frame, select = c(-PUNCT, -X, -VERB, -Sing, -Plur))

#Move letters to Atticus to the top
row.one <- final.frame[final.frame$title %in% c("Letters to Atticus"),]
other.rows <- final.frame[final.frame$title %in% c("Letters to Atticus") == FALSE,]
final.frame <- rbind(row.one, other.rows)
rownames(frame) <- 1:length(frame$title) #Replace the rownames with the new correct number
remove(other.rows)
remove(row.one)


#Clean up NAs and filter out Cicero's works that are not the Letters to Atticus
final.frame[is.na(final.frame)] <- 0

#Add a number to make

#CAN GET RID OF THIS LATER WHEN I FIX TITLES
#final.frame$title[46] <- "Apocolocyntosis"
#final.frame <- final.frame[grepl("Horace", final.frame$title) == FALSE,]

#cicero_works_nonatt <- c("M. Tulli Ciceronis Orationes", "Librorum de Re Publica Sex", "M. Tulli Ciceronis\nscripta quae manserunt omnia,\nfasc. 43", "de Natura Deorum", "M. Tullii Ciceronis opera quae supersunt omnia", "Tusculanae Disputationes", "M. Tulli Ciceronis Rhetorica, Tomus II", "De Senectute De Amicitia De Divinatione, With An English Translation", "Epistulae ad Familiares", "Letters to and from Quintus")

# #final.frame <- final.frame[(final.frame$title %in% cicero_works_nonatt) == FALSE,]
# 
# 
# #These values give NA, cause issues
# final.frame[c("Neg", "Rcp", "Art")] <- NULL

for (i in 1:length(final.frame$title)) {
  final.frame$title <- as.character(final.frame$title)
  final.frame$title[i] <- paste(as.character(i), final.frame$title[i])
}

rownames(final.frame) <- final.frame$title
final.frame$title <- lapply(X=final.frame$title, FUN=substr, start=1, stop=16)
rownames(final.frame) <- lapply(X=rownames(final.frame), FUN=substr, start=1, stop=16)

Now that we have a viable DF, let’s cut it up a little bit. We don’t want the first column, because that’s all text. Not every clustering algorithm needs the DF to be pre-scaled (that is, subtracting the mean from every value and dividing it by the standard deviation to make the comparable). However, some do expect the data to be scaled, so lets create two variables. df_clust isn’t scaled, and df_scaled is.


df_clust <- final.frame[,2:length(final.frame)]
#df_clust <- slice_sample(df_clust, n=45)

df_scaled <- as.data.frame(scale(df_clust))

Analysis

First, to get some of the formatting predefined:

#10 variables to use for analysis. This is based of the work done in the Testing section below
vars <- c("2", "PART", "Imp", "Pres", "Part", "Abl", "Pass", "Fut", "1", "Prs")
df_clust <- df_clust %>% select(all_of(vars))
rownames(df_clust) <- rownames(final.frame)
df_scaled <- df_scaled %>% select(all_of(vars))
rownames(df_scaled) <- rownames(df_scaled)

#Add this with the + operator to fviz_cluster plots. @param data is the original dataset used for cluster analysis (df_clust in the first case)
cluster_labels <- geom_text_repel(aes(label=rownames(data)), vjust="inward", hjust="inward", max.overlaps = 20, size=3)

#Args for fviz_clust
palette <- c("#2E9FDF", "#00AFBB", "#E7B800", "red","gray")
geom <- "point"
ellipse.type <- "convex"
ggtheme <- theme_bw()

#K-means
k <- 4

#Hcut
hc_func <- "agnes"
hc_method <- "ward"
hc_metric <- "euclidean"
cex <- 0.2

#Text labels for distance diagrams. @param label is usually the rownames of a data.frame
margin_size <- 10
distance_labels <- theme(axis.text = element_text(size=5, vjust = 1), margins = margin(t = margin_size, r = margin_size, b = margin_size, l = margin_size))

#Dendrogram theme
dend_size <- 0.2
dend_theme <- theme(axis.text = element_text(size=dend_size, vjust = 1), margins = margin(t = margin_size, r = margin_size, b = margin_size, l = margin_size))

K-means clustering

Time to cluster! K-means is a good starting point here. I’ll check out CLARA as well. We’ll need to evaluate what’s best. Here’s the modifications we can make:

  1. Change which variables we account for.

  2. Change the number of centers (between 3 and 5? But we already have a way of visualizing that)

  3. Change the measuring method (i.e. Euclidean

  4. Change the algorithm


kmeans_basic_cluster <- kmeans(df_scaled, centers=k, nstart=25)
kmeans_basic_table <- data.frame(kmeans_basic_cluster$size, kmeans_basic_cluster$centers)
kmeans_basic_df <- data.frame(Clusters = kmeans_basic_cluster$cluster, df_clust)

Let’s do our first visualization. Four clusters seems like a reasonable amount, looking at the fviz_nbclust results with the wss method.


#TODO ADD SUMMARIES OF THE ABOVE DATA HERE

#Principal component analysis

#pca <- prcomp(df_scaled)

#summary(pca)

#Visualizations

fviz_nbclust(df_clust, kmeans, method="wss", nstart=50)


cluster_main <- fviz_cluster(kmeans_basic_cluster, 
             data = df_clust, 
             palette = palette, 
             geom = geom,
             ellipse.type = ellipse.type, 
             ggtheme = ggtheme,
             ) + geom_text_repel(aes(label=rownames(df_clust)), vjust="inward", hjust="inward", max.overlaps = 12, size=2)
cluster_main


str(kmeans_basic_cluster)
List of 9
 $ cluster     : int [1:172] 4 3 3 3 3 3 3 3 3 3 ...
 $ centers     : num [1:4, 1:10] -0.808 0.315 -0.368 1.9 -0.122 ...
  ..- attr(*, "dimnames")=List of 2
  .. ..$ : chr [1:4] "1" "2" "3" "4"
  .. ..$ : chr [1:10] "2" "PART" "Imp" "Pres" ...
 $ totss       : num 1710
 $ withinss    : num [1:4] 113.7 117 151 56.9
 $ tot.withinss: num 439
 $ betweenss   : num 1271
 $ size        : int [1:4] 61 39 44 28
 $ iter        : int 2
 $ ifault      : int 0
 - attr(*, "class")= chr "kmeans"
distance <- get_dist(df_scaled)
fviz_dist(distance, gradient = list(low = "#00AFBB", mid = "white", high = "#FC4E07")) + distance_labels

ggsave(filename = "Images/Distance plot with main d.f.png", dpi="print")

The graph above should be the first visualization in the presentation. Next, we want to see what sets the Letters and other similar texts apart from the others.

The following graph represents the loadings for the X axis of the cluster analysis above. The farther we go to one side, the more 1st/2nd person verbs, personal pronouns, present/future tense verbs, and imperfective aspect verbs there are. Let’s talk about what that means.

Person corresponds to a change in tenor accompanied by colloquial texts. Tenor, as a term, refers to the author and their relationship to their audience. Comedy, letters, and works of Seneca which come very close to that cluster have an intimate audience (in the case of Comedy and Seneca, this is an affectation. In such a close situation, the goal is to both get information from the addressee but also communicate information about themselves.

This information about person is fairly obvious, but the situation with personal pronouns is less obvious. Latin has no need of personal pronouns; the grammars say they tend to be used for emphasis or …(cite Allen & Greenough and Kühner / Stegmann).

The information about tense is also expected. Speakers in colloquial texts have a variety of purposes: asking and answering questions, demonstrating their current thoughts and feelings, making plans, and so on. These cover a broad range of statements, each of which requires a different type of verb (examples here).

To sum up, texts on the left-hand side of the graph have a highly personal tenor with ….FILL IN HERE, RE-READ BIBER

Texts on the the right-hand side of the cluster graph feature more ablative nouns, participles, and passive verbs.

pca <- prcomp(df_scaled)

rotations.df <- as.data.frame(pca$rotation)

rotations <- data.frame(var = rownames(rotations.df), rotations = rotations.df$PC1)

ggplot(rotations, aes(var, rotations)) + geom_col()


#NOTE: I should try this with Act as a variable too. Pass alone might be high because there are a lot of participles and ablative nouns.

So, we know the correlation here, but I want to know whether these correlation values are significant. We want to see

I want to try this graph with the best combo of factors for PCA alone. I found that c(“2”, “PART”, “Imp”, “Pres”, “Part”, “Abl”) could explain a full 90% of the textual variation with the first two principal components.


df_sub <- df_scaled %>% select("2", PART, Imp, Pres, Part, Abl)
df_sub_unscaled <- df_clust %>% select("2", PART, Imp, Pres, Part, Abl)

kmeans.res <- kmeans(df_sub, centers = k, nstart = 50)

fviz_nbclust(df_sub, kmeans, method="wss", nstart = 50)


cluster_main <- fviz_cluster(kmeans.res, 
             data = df_sub, 
             palette = palette, 
             geom = geom,
             ellipse.type = ellipse.type, 
             ggtheme = ggtheme,
             ) + geom_text_repel(aes(label=rownames(df_clust)), vjust="inward", hjust="inward", max.overlaps = 12, size=2)
cluster_main

ggsave(filename = "Images/six_factors.png", dpi="print")

Let’s take a look at a data frame with the clusters added.

km.dataframe <- data.frame(clusters = kmeans.res$cluster, df_sub)
km.dataframe$title <- final.frame$title

km.unscaled <- data.frame(clusters = kmeans.res$cluster, df_sub_unscaled)
km.unscaled$title <- final.frame$title

get_means <- function(INDEX) {
  temp <- select(km.unscaled, -title)
  apply(X=temp[temp$clusters == INDEX,], MARGIN = 2, FUN=mean)
}

cluster_means <- map(unique(km.unscaled$clusters), get_means)
cluster_means
[[1]]
 clusters        X2      PART       Imp      Pres      Part       Abl 
   1.0000  249.8982  304.2403   41.3060  906.2598  538.1089 1079.4954 

[[2]]
  clusters         X2       PART        Imp       Pres       Part        Abl 
   2.00000   60.14127  199.22323   10.40872  392.14723  743.12188 1412.73457 

[[3]]
 clusters        X2      PART       Imp      Pres      Part       Abl 
   3.0000  522.5767  123.6006  124.2560 1265.6285  607.5766 1125.2081 

[[4]]
 clusters        X2      PART       Imp      Pres      Part       Abl 
   4.0000 1218.5498  196.8296  216.5851 1776.9405  312.8467  602.8821 
remove(df_sub)
remove(kmeans.res)

So, we have the cluster means for our parameters now

I commented this out because I’m done with it, but I saved the results in a data table. Because K-means has an element of randomness, the results aren’t identical every time, but Hartigan-Wong, the default algorithm, either had the lowest or tied for the lowest total wihin sum of squares.

a l gorithm

<chr>

t o t . w ithinss

<dbl>
H a r t i g an-Wong 3 988.635
Lloyd 3 989.989
Forgy 3 993.158
M acQueen 3 989.989 

# 
# algorithms <- c("Hartigan-Wong", "Lloyd", "Forgy", "MacQueen")
# 
# algorithms.tot.withinss <- vector(mode = "double")
# for (i in 1:length(algorithms)) {
#   algorithms.tot.withinss[i] <- kmeans(df_scaled, centers=k, nstart=25, algorithm = algorithms[i])$tot.withinss
# }
# 
# data.frame(algorithm = algorithms, tot.withinss = algorithms.tot.withinss)

But, let’s look at the cluster that contains the letters to Atticus. Maybe a bar chart showing facets of each?

att_clust <- kmeans_basic_df$Clusters[rownames(kmeans_basic_df) == "22 Letters t"]

#df.barplot <- data.frame(names = dimnames(kmeans_basic_df)[[2]][-1], attclust = sapply(X=kmeans_basic_df[kmeans_basic_df$Clusters == att_clust,], sum)[-1])

#df.barplot$poetclust <-  sapply(X=kmeans_basic_df[kmeans_basic_df$Clusters == 2,], sum)[-1]

df.barplot <- data.frame(cluster = 1, var = dimnames(kmeans_basic_df)[[2]][-1], clustersum = scale(sapply(X=kmeans_basic_df[kmeans_basic_df$Clusters == 1,], mean)[-1]))
for (i in 2:max(kmeans_basic_df$Clusters)) {
  temp <- data.frame(cluster = i, var = dimnames(kmeans_basic_df)[[2]][-1], clustersum = scale(sapply(X=kmeans_basic_df[kmeans_basic_df$Clusters == i,], mean)[-1]))
  df.barplot <- rbind(df.barplot, temp)
}


ggplot(data = df.barplot, aes(var, clustersum)) + labs(title = "Z-scores of mean number of tags per cluster category") + facet_wrap(~cluster) + geom_col() + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust=1, size=4))

ggsave(filename = "./Images/cluster_vals.png", dpi="print")

2/15/2026: Interesting to note that all POS’s tagged as Singular are higher in Cluster 3, which Att. belongs to. Verbs also look farthest above the mean in the same cluster, so that is worth investigating as well. Present tense is by far the most common in Cluster 3, and it only comes close in cluster 5.

  • Cluster 3 contains only comedy, Sulpicia, and the Letters to Atticus

CLARA

Clara is another clustering algorithm like K-means, but which

AGNES

But it might be a good idea to look at other methods of clustering as well. The graphs above seem to show that K-means gets some well-defined groupings, but let’s test out hcut. I do know that there are many methods of hclust, so the next step after some more experimentation is to try that.

We also want to see what creates the tightest clusters. We have a few parameters we can change:

  1. The number of variables under consideration, like above

  2. The function (hclust, agnes, or diana)

  3. The agglomeration method (ward.D2 is oft-recommended, but it’s worth seeing what works best)

  4. The metric (I’ve only seen Euclidean and Manhattan mentioned, I don’t know which is best)

Note: AGNES apparently comes with a value telling us how tight the clusters are, according to this article.


#df_clust2 <- df_clust[sample(nrow(df_clust), 80),]
df_clust2 <- df_clust #I've commented out the one above because sometimes I want a sample

hcut_results <- hcut(df_clust2, k=k, hc_func = hc_func, hc_method = hc_method) #k is defined in the first section in Analysis

Now, let’s do tests to see what the best variables to study are. It seems that principal component analysis is the way to go for this.

Here are some of the results of looking at PCA. It looks like Person, Pronouns, Adjectives, Prepositions, Particles, Personal Pronouns, Tenses, and AUX are very important. The Ablative too might be a good idea to include

  • Pass one: c(“1”, “2”, “AUX”, “Pres”, “Part”, “Prs”) = 0.88916

  • Pass two: c(“1”, “2”, “PROPN”, “Fut”, “Fin”, “Pass”) = 0.87284

  • Pass three: c(“1”, “2”, “3”, “AUX”, “Pres”, “Abl”) = 0.88998

  • Pass four: c(“1”, “ADJ”, “SCONJ”, “Pres”, “Pass”, “Prs”) = 0.87152

  • Pass five: c(“1”, “ADP”, “Fut”, “Pres”, “Part”, “Pass”) = 0.88226

  • c("2", "PART", "Imp", "Pres", "Part", "Abl") = 0.90210

This last one has the best results, but what if I add a few more? It doesn’t include Pass, Fut., first person, Prs. I’ll try it out in the PCA testing section.

c(“2”, “PART”, “Imp”, “Pres”, “Part”, “Abl”, “Pass”, “Fut”, “1”, “Prs”)

Visualization and Significance Testing

#Lets look at boxplots for each cluster:
#Cluster 4 is Att

kmeans_basic_df$Clusters <- as.factor(kmeans_basic_df$Clusters)

ggplot(kmeans_basic_df, aes(Clusters, X1)) + geom_boxplot()


ggplot(kmeans_basic_df, aes(Clusters, X2)) + geom_boxplot()


ggplot(kmeans_basic_df, aes(Clusters, Prs)) + geom_boxplot()


ggplot(kmeans_basic_df, aes(Clusters, Pres)) + geom_boxplot()


ggplot(kmeans_basic_df, aes(Clusters, PART)) + geom_boxplot()


ggplot(kmeans_basic_df, aes(Clusters, Imp)) + geom_boxplot()


ggplot(kmeans_basic_df, aes(Clusters, Part)) + geom_boxplot()


ggplot(kmeans_basic_df, aes(Clusters, Abl)) + geom_boxplot()


ggplot(kmeans_basic_df, aes(Clusters, Pass)) + geom_boxplot()


ggplot(kmeans_basic_df, aes(Clusters, Fut)) + geom_boxplot()

Lemmas

We want lemmas which are uncommon in all texts but unusually common in cluster 4.

First, we need to add cluster labels to data. Use apply? kmeans_basic_df$Clusters[kmeans_basic_df$title ==

kmeans_basic_df %>% group_by(X2) %>% mean

[1] NA

For each unique lemma:

  1. Get the

Testing

This section is for a variety of tests. I look at which parameters generate the tightest clusters in this first section, and then I select random variables and see which combinations give the highest Proportion of Variance in the first 2 principal components. I use this to try and identify the best subset of variables to look at. Then, I use PCA again, this time manually looking at the rotations and removing variables which have the lowest value. This method actually seems to have less success than my try-random-variables method, as the best PoV I could explain was in the 70s%, as opposed to the random selection was in the 80s% or even around 90%.


###TESTING WITH AGNES############################
# methods to assess
m <- c( "average", "single", "complete", "ward") #note that 'ward' = hclust's 'ward.D2
names(m) <- m

funcs <- c("agnes")
names(funcs) <- funcs

metrics <- c("euclidean", "manhattan", "maximum")
names(metrics) <- metrics



# function to compute coefficient
ac_method <- function(metric, func) {
  as.data.frame(map_df(m, ac_final, metric = metric, func = func))
}

ac_final <- function(method, metric, func){
  as.data.frame(hcut(df_clust2, hc_func = func, hc_method = method,  hc_metric = metric)$ac)
}

past_vals <- function(func, metric, method) {
  strings <- vector(mode = "character")
  for (f in 1:length(func)) {
    for (metr in 1:length(metric)) {
      for (meth in 1:length(method)) {
        strings <- append(strings, paste(func[f], metric[metr], method[meth]))
      }
    }
  }
  return(strings)
}



ac_metric <- function(func) {
  as.data.frame(map_df(metrics, ac_method, func = func))
}

ac <- map_df(funcs, ac_metric)
ac$names <- past_vals(funcs, metrics, m)
View(ac)

#This seems to show that Ward is always the best, no matter the distance measure. If we use agnes, that is.

#   average    single  complete      ward  weighted 
# 0.7851783 0.7154408 0.8671490 0.9637023 0.8008371 
#As expected, ward got the best results


#############################################
#Test groups of two variables

#Test groups of three variables

#Test groups of four variables

#Test groups of five variables

#(We'll stop there, a permutation sampling 5 from 15 already has over 300,000 variations)

#INDEX is a character vector including columns to select
get_ac <- function(INDEX) {
  agnes(df_scaled[,colnames(df_scaled) %in% INDEX], metric = "euclidean", stand = FALSE, method = "ward")$ac
}

#Start testing combinations. 

#This function accepts as a parameter the number of variables to sample from the total. It looks at every combination possible with that sample, and picks the top 25 which have the highest ac (see agnes.object in the help pages for more details). To summarize, ac = i - m(i), with i being an observation, and m(i) = d(C1) / d(Cn), with d(C1) being the dissimilarity with the first singleton cluster, and d(Cn) being the dissimilarity with the final, agglomerated cluster. The interpretation of ac is the higher the value, the better the model fit the data into clusters
lowest_spread <- function(sample_var) {
  combination <- combn(colnames(df_scaled), sample_var, simplify = FALSE)
  combination <- sample(combination, 300)
  ac_list <- lapply(X=combination, FUN=get_ac)
  names(ac_list) <- combination
  ac_vec <- unlist(ac_list)
  sort(ac_vec, decreasing = TRUE)[1:25]
}

####!NOTE that the following items will get slightly different results every time!###

#Pick groups of 2 variables
lowest_spread(2)

#Pick groups of 3 variables
three.sample <- lowest_spread(sample_var = 3)

#Pick groups of 4 variables
four.sample <- lowest_spread(4)

#Pick groups of 5 variables
five.sample <- lowest_spread(5)

#Pick groups of 6 variables
six.sample <- lowest_spread(6)

#############################################

#Now, let's check the pca and see whether we can get a higher value

get_pca <- function(INDEX) {
  pca.data <- prcomp(df_scaled[,colnames(df_scaled) %in% INDEX])
  return(summary(pca.data)$importance[3,2])
}

highest_pca <- function(sample_var) {
  combination <- combn(colnames(df_scaled), sample_var, simplify = FALSE)
  combination <- sample(combination, 300)
  ac_list <- lapply(X=combination, FUN=get_pca)
  names(ac_list) <- combination
  ac_vec <- unlist(ac_list)
  sort(ac_vec, decreasing = TRUE)[1:25]
}

six.sample <- highest_pca(6)
six.sample

seven.sample <- highest_pca(7)
seven.sample

Now that I’ve read up a bit more in PCA in R, I know that I can actually see the strength of correlation for each individual variable


pca <- prcomp(df_scaled) #NOTE: Should try making the rank. argument smaller, maybe 5?

View(pca$rotation)

pca <- df_scaled %>% select(-Rcp, -Tot, -Cmp, -Con, -Art, -Voc, -Prosp, -PROPN, -NUM) %>% prcomp
summary(pca)

View(pca$rotation)

pca <- df_scaled %>% select(-Rcp, -Tot, -Cmp, -Con, -Art, -Voc, -Prosp, -PROPN, -NUM, -Masc, -Fem, -Neut, -CCONJ) %>% prcomp
summary(pca)
View(pca$rotation)

#Combine the correlation strength from the first two principal components, let's see if we can remove more
cor <- abs(as.data.frame(pca$rotation)$PC1) + abs(as.data.frame(pca$rotation)$PC2)
names(cor) <- rownames(as.data.frame(pca$rotation))
sort(cor)

remove(cor)

#Try with just the top 10 variables
pca <- df_scaled %>% select(NOUN, PRON, Rel, Sub, Dem, Past) %>% prcomp
summary(pca)
View(pca$rotation)

pca <- df_scaled %>% select(-Rcp, -Tot, -Cmp, -Con, -Art, -Voc, -Prosp, -PROPN, -NUM, -Masc, -Fem, -Neut, -CCONJ, -ADV, -Acc, -Loc, -Dat, -Nom) %>% prcomp
summary(pca)
View(pca$rotation)

pca <- df_scaled %>% select(c("2", "PART", "Imp", "Pres", "Part", "Abl", "Pass", "Fut", "1", "Prs")) %>% prcomp(rank. = 2)
summary(pca)
View(pca$rotation)

#Try with the same variables as above
#df_test <- df_scaled %>% select(-Rcp, -Tot, -Cmp, -Con, -Art, -Voc, -Prosp, -PROPN, -NUM, -Masc, -Fem, -Neut, -CCONJ, -ADV, -Acc, -Loc, -Dat, -Nom) 

#Try this with a new subset of variables
df_test <- df_scaled %>% select(c("2", "PART", "Imp", "Pres", "Part", "Abl", "Pass", "Fut", "1", "Prs"))

fviz_cluster(kmeans(df_test, centers = k, nstart = 50), data=df_test, stand = FALSE, palette = c("red", "green", "blue", "purple", "yellow", "magenta", "gray", "cyan"), 
             geom = geom,
             ellipse.type = ellipse.type, 
             ggtheme = ggtheme,
             ) + geom_text_repel(aes(label=rownames(df_clust)), vjust="inward", hjust="inward", max.overlaps = 12, size=2) 

km.test <- kmeans(df_test, centers = 8, nstart = 50)
df.test <- data.frame(clusters = km.test$cluster, df_test)
df.test$title <- final.frame$title


remove(km.test)
remove(df.test)


ggsave(filename = "Images/pca_test_cluster.png", dpi="print")

Given the previous section, let’s only remove the following items:

  • VERB

  • CCONJ

  • X

  • NOUN

  • ADJ

  • Sing

  • Plur

  • Act

  • All cases but Abl

  • Degree

  • Neg

fviz_nbclust(df_clust2, hcut, method="wss")
fviz_nbclust(df_clust2, hcut, method = "silhouette")
fviz_nbclust(df_clust2, hcut, method="gap_stat")

agnes_results <- hcut(df_scaled[,colnames(df_scaled) %in% c("ADP", "2", "Pres", "Fin", "Pass", "Gen")], stand = FALSE, k = 5, hc_func = hc_func, hc_method = hc_method, hc_metric = hc_metric)

fviz_dend(agnes_results, palette = palette, 
          geom = geom,
          ellipse.type = ellipse.type,
          type = "rectangle",
          rect=TRUE,
          #horiz=TRUE,
          ggtheme = theme_bw(),
          cex = cex)

POS, VERBS, etc.

Note that these analyses are old, older than when I did my tests with PCA. It seems that the variables I’ve settled on explain the variance better than just POS or just Verbs or categories like that.

summary(pca)

Importance of first k=2 (out of 10) components:
                          PC1    PC2
Standard deviation     2.6730 1.1200
Proportion of Variance 0.7145 0.1254
Cumulative Proportion  0.7145 0.8399

Note in the following how the Letters to Atticus are quite far away from Comedy and Petronius! Unlike the previous, when looking at POS tags.

summary(pca)

Importance of first k=2 (out of 10) components:
                          PC1    PC2
Standard deviation     2.6730 1.1200
Proportion of Variance 0.7145 0.1254
Cumulative Proportion  0.7145 0.8399
scaled_verb <- scale(df_verb)
kmeans_basic_cluster <- kmeans(scaled_verb, centers=5, nstart=20)
kmeans_basic_table <- data.frame(kmeans_basic_cluster$size, kmeans_basic_cluster$centers)
kmeans_basic_df <- data.frame(Clusters = kmeans_basic_cluster$cluster, df_verb)

#TODO ADD SUMMARIES OF THE ABOVE DATA HERE

pca <- prcomp(scaled_verb)

summary(pca)

#Visualizations

choose <- NULL

fviz_nbclust(scaled_verb, kmeans, method="wss", nstart=50)

fviz_cluster(kmeans_basic_cluster, choose.vars = choose, data = df_verb, palette = palette, 
             geom = "point",
             ellipse.type = "convex", 
             ggtheme = theme_bw(),
             ) + geom_text_repel(aes(label=rownames(df_clust)))

When looking at Case, it seems that Comedy, Seneca, Petronius, and the Letters to Atticus cluster together again. It is a strong enough cluster that they nearly form their own category.

summary(pca)

Importance of first k=2 (out of 10) components:
                          PC1    PC2
Standard deviation     2.6730 1.1200
Proportion of Variance 0.7145 0.1254
Cumulative Proportion  0.7145 0.8399
scaled_case <- scale(df_case)
kmeans_basic_cluster <- kmeans(scaled_case, centers=6, nstart=20)
kmeans_basic_table <- data.frame(kmeans_basic_cluster$size, kmeans_basic_cluster$centers)
kmeans_basic_df <- data.frame(Clusters = kmeans_basic_cluster$cluster, df_case)

#TODO ADD SUMMARIES OF THE ABOVE DATA HERE

pca <- prcomp(scaled_case)

summary(pca)

#Visualizations

choose <- NULL

fviz_nbclust(scaled_case, kmeans, method="wss", nstart=50)

fviz_cluster(kmeans_basic_cluster, choose.vars = choose, data = df_case, palette = c(palette, "brown"), 
             geom = "point",
             ellipse.type = "convex", 
             ggtheme = theme_bw(),
             ) + geom_text(aes(label=rownames(df_clust)))

Notes on Specific Tags

I want to see whether some of my observations from the thesis bear out. For example, what if we only look at participles? Here are the lowest 25 texts by participle count:

final.frame %>% slice_min(Part, n=25) %>% select(title) %>% print(n = 25)

Notice how it’s just comedy and the letters to Atticus!!!

But what about other letters? Let’s look at Pliny’s letters

final.frame %>% 
  slice_min(Part, n=229) %>% 
  select(title) %>% 
  print(n = 229)

Misc Tests

I want to understand K-means clustering better, so I’m gonna play around with it here


frame <- data.frame(a=rnorm(10, mean = 10), b=rnorm(10, mean = 20))

scaled <- scale(frame)

scaled

km_result <- kmeans(scaled, centers = 2, nstart = 5)
km_table <- data.frame(km_result$cluster, km_result$centers)

fviz_cluster(km_result, data = scaled, palette = c("gray", "red"), 
             geom = "point",
             ellipse.type = "convex", 
             ggtheme = theme_bw(),
             )

And some ggplot2 experiments based on the book. Here’s some of my playing around with ch. 8.

df <- data.frame(x = 1, y = 3:1, family = c("sans", "serif", "mono"))

df

ggplot(df, aes(x, y)) + 
  geom_text(aes(label = family, family = family))

fviz_cluster(kmeans_basic_cluster, data = df_clust, palette = c("#2E9FDF", "#00AFBB", "#E7B800", "red","gray"), 
             geom = "point",
             ellipse.type = "convex", 
             ggtheme = theme_bw(),
             ) + geom_text_repel(aes(label=rownames(df_clust)), vjust="inward", hjust="inward", check_overlap = TRUE, size=3)

fviz_dend(hcut_results, palette = c("#2E9FDF", "#00AFBB", "#E7B800", "red"), 
          geom = "point",
          ellipse.type = "convex",
          type = "rectangle",
          #type = "phylogenic",
          #repel = TRUE,
          rect = TRUE,
          cex = 0.4,
          ggtheme = theme_bw()
)

fviz_dend(hcut_results, k=4, palette = c("#2E9FDF", "#00AFBB", "#E7B800", "red"), 
          ellipse.type = "convex",
          type = "phylogenic",
          repel = TRUE,
          cex = 0.7,
          ggtheme = theme_bw()
)

Some crucial notes on the previous section:

  • Adding the check_overlap argument to geom_text() in fviz_cluster() is very useful. It removes overlapping labels, privileging those that are higher in the data frame (meaning the letters to Atticus are usually visible)

  • Reduce cex to 0.7 for readability

How K-Means Clustering Works

K-Means clustering itself is relatively simple. The computer has a set of data, and it wants to determine which observations are closer to each other than another.

Let’s first imagine we’re talking about actual distance

Point X Coordinate Y Coordinate
A 1 0
B 2 0
C 1 10
D 2 10
E 3 10

Let’s graph these points:


df <- data.frame(X.Coordinate = c(1,2,1,2,3), Y.Coordinate = c(0, 0, 10, 10, 10), row.names = LETTERS[1:5])

ggplot(data = df, aes(x = X.Coordinate, y = Y.Coordinate)) + geom_point()

Okay, so we want the computer to recognize that there are two groups of points close together, the ones at the top and the bottom.

The K-means algorithm lets the computer recognize there are two clusters.

km <- kmeans(df, centers = 2, nstart = 5)

fviz_cluster(km, data = df)

But we’re not looking at distances, we’re looking at data. However, we can apply the same method to data that doesn’t represent an actual distance.

Let’s say we have a bunch of students, and each take two classes. We want to sort the students into groups, but how do we do that when they have two grades in different classes? We could do it by eye, but what if they were taking 4 classes? 5 classes? How can we objectively sort them into groups?

Starting with the simplest case, here are some grades:

Student English Math
Sally 95 89
Susan 96 72
Sam 55 78
Shelly 76 75
Stu 40 96

We can plot these values in space, even though they are


df <- data.frame(English = c(95, 96, 55, 76, 40), Math = c(89, 72, 78, 75, 96), row.names = c("Sally", "Susan", "Sam", "Shelly", "Stu"))

ggplot(df, aes(x = English, y = Math)) + geom_point() + geom_text(aes(label = rownames(df)), nudge_y = -2)

Well, let’s do the same clustering algorithm again.

NOTE: I tried it again without scaling, the results are similar, but different. Now, Stu is matched up with Sam. (The eagle-eyed among you might notice there’s an extra step in here. If you’re curious, we needed to scale the values (subtract the mean and divide by the standard deviation) so that they’re comparable with each other.)


#scaled_df = scale(df)

km <- kmeans(df, centers = 2, nstart = 2)

fviz_cluster(km, data = df)

Notice how the other students are similar to each other because they’re scores in their two classes are pretty close together. Stu, however, has one high and one low value (he really doesn’t like English), so he’s very different from the others.

R is great because it also handles data with more than two dimensions. If each student had many classes (Math, English, History, Science, Music, Art), then we could use principal component analysis to plot differences between these datasets in 2D space. That way, K-means works even for large datasets.
